---
title: "Introduction to robotstxtr"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Introduction to robotstxtr}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r setup}
library(robotstxtr)
```

## What robotstxtr does

`robotstxtr` decides whether a URL may be crawled according to a site's
`robots.txt` rules. It answers one question — *"is this URL allowed for this
crawler?"* — and answers it exactly the way Google's own crawler would, because
the matching engine is a vendored, upstream-test-validated snapshot of Google's
open-source C++ `robots.txt` parser and matcher.

The core value is **fidelity**. `robots.txt` matching has many quirks — longest
rule wins, `Allow` beats `Disallow` on an equal-length tie, `*` and `$`
wildcards, case-sensitive paths but case-insensitive user-agent groups,
percent-escape canonicalization. Re-implementing those rules by hand is a common
source of subtle bugs. `robotstxtr` sidesteps that by running the real Google
algorithm and returning a stable, machine-readable decision for every URL.

This is a faithful, upstream-test-validated replica of the pinned open-source
matcher. It is not a claim of universal behavioral identity with Googlebot,
which has crawl policy beyond this matcher.

## The two entry points

There are two ways to get a decision, depending on whether you already have the
`robots.txt` text:

- `allowed_by_robots_text()` — you supply the `robots.txt` body yourself. No
  network access is performed. This is the pure matching path.
- `allowed_by_robots_url()` — you supply only the target URL(s). The package
  works out the governing `robots.txt` origin, fetches it under a deterministic
  and conservative HTTP policy, and matches.

Both return an S3 `robots_decisions` object with one row per input URL.

## Matching against a supplied body

`allowed_by_robots_text()` needs a `robots.txt` body, one or more URLs, and a
crawler user-agent token. Everything below runs offline, so it is safe to
evaluate in this vignette.

```{r}
robots <- "user-agent: *
disallow: /private
allow: /private/public
"

allowed_by_robots_text(robots, "https://example.com/private/report", "mybot")
```

The result is a data-frame-backed object. Pull out the pieces you need from its
`results` table:

```{r}
decision <- allowed_by_robots_text(
  robots,
  c(
    "https://example.com/index.html",     # not disallowed, allowed
    "https://example.com/private/report", # under /private, disallowed
    "https://example.com/private/public"  # re-allowed by the Allow rule
  ),
  "mybot"
)

decision$results[c("url", "allowed", "decision_source")]
```

`allowed` is the headline `TRUE`/`FALSE`/`NA` answer. `decision_source` explains
*why*: `rule_disallow`, `rule_allow`, `default_allow` (matching ran and nothing
matched), `missing_allow` (the file was absent), or one of the `*_unknown`
values when no safe decision was possible.

### Vectorized, with per-URL user agents

A scalar user agent expands across every URL, or you can pass one user agent per
URL. Invalid rows (a missing URL, an empty user agent) never abort the call;
they come back as a per-row `input_unknown` decision in their original position.

```{r}
allowed_by_robots_text(
  "user-agent: newsbot\ndisallow: /\nuser-agent: *\nallow: /",
  c("https://example.com/a", "https://example.com/a"),
  c("newsbot", "otherbot")
)$results[c("url", "user_agent", "allowed")]
```

### Rich match metadata

For a matched row the object also records which rule won: the one-based
`matched_line`, the `matched_rule_type` (`allow`/`disallow`), and the canonical
`matched_rule_value` — the exact directive value the matcher used, after
upstream escaping canonicalization.

```{r}
m <- allowed_by_robots_text(
  "user-agent: *\ndisallow: /*.pdf$",
  "https://example.com/manual.pdf",
  "mybot"
)
cols <- c("allowed", "matched_line", "matched_rule_type", "matched_rule_value")
m$results[cols]
```

### Inspecting the stored body

The supplied (or fetched) `robots.txt` body is stored once per source. Preview
or extract it with `robots_body()`:

```{r}
robots_body(decision, n = 40)
```

## Fetching robots.txt for a URL

When you do not already have the `robots.txt` text, `allowed_by_robots_url()`
does the whole job: it derives the `scheme://host[:port]/robots.txt` origin for
each URL (handling IDN hosts, ports, IPv6 literals, and userinfo stripping via
`rurl`), fetches each distinct origin exactly once under a conservative `httr2`
policy (10-second timeout, at most five redirects, no HTTPS-to-HTTP downgrades,
no cookies or credentials forwarded), and matches every URL against the body it
retrieved.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
# Not evaluated here: this call performs a network request, which vignettes
# must never require. Run it in an interactive session against a real site.
allowed_by_robots_url("https://example.com/some/page", "mybot")
```

The returned object has exactly the same `robots_decisions` shape as
`allowed_by_robots_text()`, with additional fetch metadata populated per row:
`fetch_outcome` (for example `fetched`, `missing`, `redirect_error`,
`timeout`), `http_status`, and `robots_url`. A `404`/`410` origin yields
`missing_allow` (`TRUE`, because an absent file allows everything); any fetch
failure yields `fetch_unknown` (`NA`), so a transport problem never silently
looks like an allow.

If you only want the fetch stage without matching, `robots_fetch()` returns the
grouped fetch results on their own.

## The URL you pass is the URL that is matched

`robots.txt` matching is sensitive to the exact URL bytes: path case, query
strings, fragments, and percent escapes all matter. `robotstxtr` passes your
original URL string to the matcher unchanged — it never cleans, decodes, or
reserializes it. The upstream matcher expects an appropriately encoded, RFC 3986
`%`-escaped full URL, so supplying the URL in that form is the caller's
responsibility.

```{r}
# Path case is significant: /Private is disallowed, /private is not.
allowed_by_robots_text(
  "user-agent: *\ndisallow: /Private",
  c("https://example.com/Private", "https://example.com/private"),
  "mybot"
)$results[c("url", "allowed")]
```

## Summary

- `allowed_by_robots_text()` matches against a body you supply, fully offline.
- `allowed_by_robots_url()` fetches and matches under a deterministic policy.
- Both return a stable, vectorized `robots_decisions` object with `allowed`,
  `decision_source`, fetch metadata, and per-rule match metadata.
- Decisions come from a vendored snapshot of Google's own matcher, so the quirky
  parts of `robots.txt` semantics are handled faithfully.
